elements.sent-2411-001: This book aims to provide a self-contained introduction to the regularity theory for integro-differential elliptic equations, mostly developed in the 21st century.
elements.sent-2411-002: Such a class of equations often arises in analysis, probability theory, mathematical physics, and in several contexts in applied sciences.
elements.sent-2411-003: The authors give a detailed presentation of all the necessary techniques, primarily focusing on the main ideas rather than proving all results in their greatest generality.
elements.sent-2411-004: The book starts from the very basics, studying the square root of the Laplacian and weak solutions to linear equations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Then, the authors develop the theory of viscosity solutions to nonlinear equations and prove the main known results in this context.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, they study obstacle problems for integro-differential operators and establish the regularity of solutions and free boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-007: Almost all the covered material appears in book form for the first time, and several proofs are different (and shorter) than those in the original papers.
elements.sent-2411-008: Moreover, several open problems are listed throughout the book.
elements.sent-2411-009: The aim of this book is to study nonlocal equations of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq-preface} \tag{$\ast$} \int_{\R^n}\bigl(u(x)-u(x+y)\bigr)K(y)dy=0\qquad for\quad x\in\Omega\subset\R^n,\end{equation} with kernels $K\geq0$.
elements.sent-2411-010: These equations are elliptic, and they share many properties with elliptic PDE: maximum principle, existence and uniqueness of solutions, regularity results, etc. The equations we will study are of type \eqref{eq-preface , with a kernel $K(y)$ that is not integrable at the origin, while nice and integrable at infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The first and simplest example is \begin{equation}K(y)=\frac{1}{|y|^{n+1}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this particular case, the operator in \eqref{eq-preface is (a multiple of) the square root of the Laplacian $\sqrt{-\Delta}$.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Since the kernel $K$ is not integrable at the origin, the operator in \eqref{eq-preface is in some sense differentiating the function $u$, and this is why it is called an integro-differential equation.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Such a type of equations appears in several contexts, and has been studied for many years in:.
elements.sent-2411-016: Fluid mechanics (for example, in the SQG equation, or even the Boltzmann equation).
elements.sent-2411-019: From an analytical point of view, the general theory for such equations has been mostly developed in the 21st century, including both linear and nonlinear equations.
elements.sent-2411-020: This area of research has attracted great interest in the PDE community in the last 15 years, especially since the first works of Caffarelli and Silvestre on the topic.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, there is no book yet providing a systematic study of the (existence and) regularity properties of solutions to general integro-differential elliptic equations.
elements.sent-2411-022: The goal of this book is to fill this void by developing the regularity theory for (both linear and nonlinear) integro-differential elliptic operators of order $2s$, with $s\in(0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-023: As we will see, there is a strong parallelism with the theory of elliptic PDE, which corresponds to the limiting case $s=1$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Still, quite often the proofs of the results for $s\in(0,1)$ are completely independent of the ``local case'' $s=1$, and new ideas and techniques have been developed in order to treat such nonlocal equations.
elements.sent-2411-025: Most of the material we present here is accessible in book form for the first time, and we provide several simplified proofs compared to the original papers.
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, we establish some new results as well, which generalize or unify previously known results.
elements.sent-2411-027: For example, the interior regularity theory for linear operators that we develop in Chapter~\ref{ch:2 includes for the first time the most general scale-invariant class of operators of order $2s$, the existence and uniqueness of viscosity solutions in Chapter~\ref{ch:fully_nonlinear is established for a very wide class of domains, and the boundary Harnack inequality in Chapter~\ref{ch:obst_pb is proved here under a very mild assumption on the domain $\Omega$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: We hope that this book will be useful to some of the many active researchers working in this field, while at the same time being self-contained and accessible to graduate students interested in this topic.
elements.sent-2411-029: We wish to thank Gerd Grubb, Joaquim Serra, and Marvin Weidner for several comments and suggestions on this book.
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we acknowledge the support from the following funding agencies.. X.F.
elements.chapter-2411-002: The square root of the Laplacian
elements.sent-2411-031: This operator, when acting on a smooth function $u\in C^\infty_c(\R^n)$, can be defined as follows:\index{Square root of the Laplacian \begin{equation} {\sqrtl} u(x) := c_n P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy, \end{equation} for some positive constant $c_n$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Diffusive processes form the backbone of the quantitative study of many-particle systems.
elements.sent-2411-033: The mathematical description of the Brownian motion has a vast range of applications that basically touch any area in which a macroscopic description of microscopic phenomena is needed.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the last decades, however, there has been increasing observational evidence that many systems that were previously thought to behave like ordinary diffusion processes are actually better explained by assuming an anomalous behavior of the forming particles.
elements.sent-2411-035: Namely, the Brownian motion is formulated under the assumption that the movement of such particles is continuous or, alternatively, that in short time intervals it can be described (thanks to the central limit theorem) by means of Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, such a description fails to account for the situations in which the underlying random variables have infinite variance.
elements.sent-2411-037: This is precisely the setting where anomalous diffusion appears, the Cauchy process being a first important example.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this setting, particles can jump towards another point and the increments of the process follow a Cauchy distribution (see Section~\ref{sec:Levy for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Macroscopically, the evolution of the density of particles is not only characterized by its local profile, but rather it is influenced by the whole distribution (being further objects less relevant).
elements.sent-2411-040: In terms of the corresponding transition function, the operator governing the evolution of a given density is no longer a local operator (contrary to the Brownian motion, where we find the Laplacian $-\Delta$), but instead we find the square root of the Laplacian, denoted by $\sqrtl$, a nonlocal operator.
elements.sent-2411-041: More precisely, the evolution of a density of particles is governed by the fractional heat equation: \begin{equation}\partial_t u + \sqrtl u = 0\quadin\quad \R^n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-042: This type of nonlocal equations have received an increasing amount of attention in the last decades, mainly motivated and driven by numerous applications: starting from the observation of Mandelbrot in the 1960s, on the deviation of stock prices from normality, \cite{Man63 , they also appear in physics, \cite{MK, MK2, Las00, ZK94 , ecology and biology, \cite{RR09, Hum10, Kla90, Vis96 , and finance, \cite{Merton, Sch03, AB88 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: As we will see, the square root of the Laplacian $\sqrtl$ can also be interpreted as the Dirichlet-to-Neumann map for the Laplace equation in the half-space (see Section~\ref{sec:harm_extension ).}
elements.sent-2411-044: As such, this operator appears in fluid mechanics, for example in the surface quasi-geostrophic equation, which is used to model the temperature on the surface of a fluid in oceanography, \cite{Con,CCV,CV , or the Benjamin-Ono equation \cite{Ben67, Ono75 , \begin{equation}\sqrtl u = -u+u^2\quadin\quad \R,\end{equation} used to describe one-dimensional internal waves in deep water, \cite{AT91, FL , and which plays an important role in the understanding of the gravity of water waves' equations in dimensions 2 and 3 \cite{GMS12 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The same operator $\sqrtl$ arises in elasticity, too: the Peierls-Nabarro equation is a model in crystal dislocation to study microscopical deformations of a material, \cite{BV, Tol97, Lu05, CS-M, DPV15 ; and the Signorini or thin obstacle problem can be used to model the equilibrium configuration of an elastic body on a frictionless surface \cite{Sig59, F-survey (see Section~\ref{sec:motivations ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: More generally, we find nonlocal operators such as $\sqrtl$ when trying to model phenomena that takes into account long-range interactions of a given system: in many materials the stress points depend on the strains of surrounding regions, \cite{Kro67, Eri72 , and nonlocal forces have been observed to propagate along fibers or laminae in composite materials \cite{INW81,Dru03, MD04 .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: On the other hand, in quantum mechanics, nonlocal operators like $\sqrtl$ appear as the relativistic momentum operator.
elements.sent-2411-048: In particular, this operator arises in dispersive equations describing the dynamics and gravitational collapse of boson stars, \cite{LY87, ES07 , or in the study of stability of relativistic matter, \cite{FLS08,Lieb ; see also \cite{FL,Las00, Las02, Sei09 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: This kind of operators appears as well in image processing, where nonlocal denoising algorithms are able to detect patterns and contours in a better way than PDE-based models, and can be used for image reconstruction and enhancement \cite{Yar85, GO08, ELB08, KFEA09, Zha10, DSL12, Yue16 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Fractional powers of the Laplacian also appear naturally in conformal geometry, where the fractional Paneitz operators are conformally covariant operators which encode geometric and topological information about the manifold; see \cite{geometry0, geometry1,Geometry2,Mar .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, we refer to \cite{mwiki,CR,CRSire,CRSavin,CSVaz,DG,DGLZ,Eri02,PK,Serfaty1,IS,IS2,IS3,Oskendal,SiICM,S-Boltzmann,Vaz,ZD for other models and motivations to study nonlocal operators like the square root of the Laplacian.}
elements.sent-2411-052: We next start studying the basic properties of $\sqrt{-\Delta}$, which will serve as an introduction to integro-differential operators and their corresponding elliptic equations.
elements.sent-2411-053: The square root of the Laplacian may be defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:square_root_Laplacian}\index{Square root of the Laplacian} \begin{split} {\sqrtl} u(x) := c_n P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy = c_n P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(z)}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dz, \end{split}\end{equation} where $c_n$ is a positive constant, given by \eqref{eq:cn below.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:square_root_Laplacian}\index{Square root of the Laplacian}  {\sqrtl} u(x) := c_n P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy = c_n P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(z)}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dz, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-054: The notation $\mathrm{P.V.}$ stands for Principal Value, which is a way (coming from distribution theory) to assign values to an integral of an a priori not absolutely integrable function.
elements.sent-2411-055: Indeed, observe that, when $u$ is smooth, $u(x) - u(x+y) = \nabla u (0)\cdot y + O(|y|^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-056: If $\nabla u$ is nonzero, then $|u(x) - u(x+y)|\cdot |y|^{-n-1}$ is comparable to $\frac{1 {|y|^n}$, which is not integrable at the origin! That is, the function to be integrated\footnote{Integrability at infinity is not a problem at this point, we can assume $u$ to be compactly supported.. is not~$L^1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The integral is then taken in principal value sense, which takes advantage of cancellations to assign a value to the integral: \begin{equation}\index{Principal Value} P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy := \lim_{\eps \downarrow 0} \int_{\R^n\setminus B_\eps} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\index{Principal Value} P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy := \lim_{\eps \downarrow 0} \int_{\R^n\setminus B_\eps} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-058: Notice that, with this definition, by symmetry we have \begin{equation}{\rm P.V.} \int_{B_1}\frac{\nabla u \cdot y }{|y |^{n+1}}\, dy = 0,\end{equation} and hence the integral in \eqref{eq:square_root_Laplacian is well defined for any $u\in C^\infty_c(\R^n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The square root of the Laplacian \eqref{eq:square_root_Laplacian is a nonlocal operator: when acting on $u$ at a point $x\in \R^n$, it uses information about $u$ that is far from~$x$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Also, we have observed that the kernel $\frac{1 {|y|^{n+1 }$ is singular (not integrable) at the origin, and hence it requires a certain regularity from $u$ near $x\in \R^n$ in order to evaluate $\sqrtl u(x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The singularity of the kernel makes it such that the operator ${\sqrtl}$ is ``differentiating'', in some sense, the function $u$, and this is why it is called an integro-differential operator.
elements.sent-2411-062: Sometimes, it is convenient to write the following alternative expression for~\eqref{eq:square_root_Laplacian : \begin{equation}\label{eq:square_root_Laplacian_incr_quotients} {\sqrtl} u(x) = \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{\R^n} \frac{2 u(x) - u(x+y)-u(x-y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Now there is no need to write $P.V.$, since the numerator of the integrand is a second order incremental quotient, and thus it has norm comparable to $|y|^2$ (and $|y|^{-n+1}$ is integrable in $\R^n$ around the origin).
elements.sent-2411-064: In particular, this expression implies that in order to evaluate ${\sqrtl}$ it is enough to assume $u\in C^2(\R^n)\cap L^\infty(\R^n)$: \begin{equation} \big|{\sqrtl} u(x)\big| \le \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{B_1}\frac{\|D^2u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}}{|y|^{n-1}}\, dy + \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1} \frac{4\|u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy \le C \left(\|u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}+\|D^2 u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}\right), \end{equation} for some $C$ depending only on $n$.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation} \big|{\sqrtl} u(x)\big| \le \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{B_1}\frac{\|D^2u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}}{|y|^{n-1}}\, dy + \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1} \frac{4\|u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy \le C \left(\|u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}+\|D^2 u\|_{L^\infty(\R^n)}\right), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-065: The regularity of $u$ takes care of the integrability of the kernel around the origin.
elements.sent-2411-066: On the other hand, when evaluating the square root of the Laplacian at a point $x\in\R^n$, it is also essential to have a global integrability assumption on $u$ to account for the contributions far from $x$.
elements.sent-2411-067: In the previous inequality, this global integrability assumption is given by the fact that $u\in L^\infty(\R^n)$.
elements.sent-2411-068: More generally, though, it is enough to consider the following space: \begin{defi \index{L1w@$L^1_\omega$} We say that $v\in L^1_\omega(\R^n)$ if \begin{equation}\|v\|_{L^1_\omega(\R^n)} := \int_{\R^n}\frac{|v(y)|}{1+|y|^{n+1}}\, dy < +\infty.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Indeed, we can split into two terms, \begin{equation} {\sqrtl} u(x) = c_n P.V.\int_{B_1} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy + c_n \int_{\R^n\setminus B_1} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy. \end{equation} Then, in order to evaluate the first term we just need a local regularity assumption on $u$ around $x$, whereas the second term is bounded (in absolute value) by $\|u\|_{L^1_\omega(\R^n)}$.
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation} {\sqrtl} u(x) = c_n P.V.\int_{B_1} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy + c_n \int_{\R^n\setminus B_1} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: Then ${\sqrtl u(0)$ is well-defined, and \begin{equation}\label{eq:flsat0} \big|{\sqrtl}u (0)\big| \le C \left(\|u\|_{C^{1,\alpha}(B_1)} + \|u\|_{L^1_\omega(\R^n)}\right)\end{equation} for some $C$ depending only on $n$ and $\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Moreover, ${\sqrtl u \in C_{\rm loc ^{0,\alpha (B_1)$ and \begin{equation}\big\|{\sqrtl} u\big\|_{C^{0,\alpha}(B_{1/2})} \le C \left(\|u\|_{C^{1,\alpha}(B_1)} + \|u\|_{L_\omega^1(\R^n)} \right)\end{equation} for some $C$ depending only on $n$ and $\alpha$.. \end{lem.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: By considering $v = u/{C_\circ}$ with $C_\circ = \|u\|_{C^{1,\alpha (B_1) + \|u\|_{L^1_\omega(\R^n)}$ we may assume that \begin{equation}\label{eq:weassume1} \|u\|_{C^{1,\alpha}(B_1)} + \|u\|_{L^1_\omega(\R^n)} = 1.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Moreover, this implies \begin{equation}\big|2u(x)-u(x+y)-u(x-y)\big|\le 2|y|^{1+\alpha}\end{equation} for all $x\in B_1$ and $y\in B_{1-|x|}$ (see \eqref{eq:proc_as_in ).}
elements.sent-2411-074: From \eqref{eq:square_root_Laplacian_incr_quotients , and integrating separately in $B_1$ and $\R^n\setminus B_1$ (recall \eqref{eq:weassume1 ), \begin{equation}\big|{\sqrtl} u(0)\big| \le C\int_{B_1}\frac{dy}{|y|^{n-\alpha}} + C\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1} \frac{|u(0)|+|u(y)|+|u(-y)|}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Observe that now, on the one hand (again using \eqref{eq:weassume1 ), \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1}\frac{|u(0)|}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy \le C\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1}\frac{dy}{|y|^{n+1}}\le C\end{equation} for some $C$ depending only on $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: And on the other hand, \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1}\frac{|u(y)|+|u(-y)|}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy = 2\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1}\frac{|u(y)|}{1+| y|^{n+1}} \frac{1+| y|^{n+1}}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy \le 4,\end{equation} where we are using that $1+|y|^{n+1}\le 2|y|^{n+1}$ since $|y|\ge 1$, and $\|u\|_{L^1_\omega(\R^n)}\le 1$.
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1}\frac{|u(y)|+|u(-y)|}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy = 2\int_{\R^n\setminus B_1}\frac{|u(y)|}{1+| y|^{n+1}} \frac{1+| y|^{n+1}}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy \le 4,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-078: More generally, repeating for any point $z\in B_{1/2}$ (and taking integrals in $B_{1/2}$ instead of $B_1$) we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:sqrtlLinf} \big\|{\sqrtl} u\big\|_{L^\infty(B_{1/2})} \le C\end{equation} as we wanted.
elements.sent-2411-079: Let us now take a cut-off function $\eta\in C^\infty_c(\R^n)$ such that $\eta \ge 0$, $\eta \equiv 0$ in $\R^n \setminus B_{3/4}$ and $\eta\equiv 1$ in $B_{2/3}$; and let us define $u_1 := \eta u$ compactly supported in $B_{3/4}$, and $u_2 := u-u_1 = (1-\eta) u$ such that $u_2 \equiv 0$ in $B_{2/3}$.

elements.sent-2411-083: If we let now $x\in B_{1/2}$ and $r := |x|$, we split \begin{equation} \sqrtl u_1(x) = \frac{c_n}{2} \int_{B_r} \frac{2u_1(x) -u_1(x+y)-u_1(x-y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy \quad +c_n \int_{\R^n\setminus B_r} \frac{u_1(x) -u_1(x+y)}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy, \end{equation} so that \begin{equation} \left|\sqrtl u_1(x)+\sqrtl u_1(-x) - 2\sqrtl u_1(0)\right| \leq \qquad \le C\int_{B_r} |y|^{\alpha-n}\, dy + C\int_{\R^n\setminus B_r} \frac{|x|^{1+\alpha}}{|y|^{n+1}}\, dy \le C r^\alpha, \end{equation} where we used \begin{equation} |u_1(x+y)+u_1(x-y)-2u_1(x)| \le C |y|^{1+\alpha}, |u_1(x)+u_1(-x)-2u_1(0)| \le C |x|^{1+\alpha}, |u_1(x+y)+u_1(-x+y)-2u_1(y)| \le C |x|^{1+\alpha}. \end{equation} Repeating it around any point in $B_{1/2}$ and together with the $L^\infty$ bound, we get $\|\sqrtl u_1\|_{C^{0,\alpha (B_{1/2 ) \le C$ by Lemma~\ref{it:H7 .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-085: Given any $x_1, x_2 \in B_{1/2}$, since $u_2 \equiv 0$ in~$B_{2/3}$, \begin{equation} \left|\sqrtl u_2(x_1)-\sqrtl u_2(x_2)\right| = \qquad = c_n \left|\int_{D} u_2(z) \left(\frac{1}{|z-x_1|^{n+1}}-\frac{1}{|z-x_2|^{n+1}}\right)\, dz\right|, \end{equation} where $D$ can be taken to be $\R^n\setminus (B_{1/6 (x_1)\cup B_{1/6 (x_2))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Observe that, in this domain, \begin{equation}\left|\frac{1}{|z-x_1|^{n+1}}-\frac{1}{|z-x_2|^{n+1}}\right|\le C\frac{|x_1-x_2|}{1+|z|^{n+2}}\quadfor all\quad z \in D,\end{equation} by regularity of $|z|^{-n-1}$ outside of the origin.

elements.sent-2411-089: We also observe that the operator ${\sqrtl}$ is positive definite: \begin{lem}\index{Integration by parts!Square root of the Laplacian Let $u, v \in C^\infty_c(\R^n)$.. Then \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n} u(x) {\sqrtl} v(x)\, dx = \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{\R^n}\int_{\R^n} \frac{\left(u(x) - u(z)\right)\left(v(x)-v(z)\right)}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dx\, dz.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-090: In particular, \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n} u(x) {\sqrtl} u(x)\, dx = \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{\R^n}\int_{\R^n} \frac{\left(u(x) - u(z)\right)^2}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dx\, dz \ge 0,\end{equation} with strict inequality unless $u\equiv 0$.
elements.formula-2411-028: \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n} u(x) {\sqrtl} u(x)\, dx = \frac{c_n}{2}\int_{\R^n}\int_{\R^n} \frac{\left(u(x) - u(z)\right)^2}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dx\, dz \ge 0,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-092: By definition, \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n} u(x) {\sqrtl} v(x)\, dx =c_n \int_{\R^n} u(x) P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{v(x) - v(z)}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dz\, dx.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n} u(x) {\sqrtl} v(x)\, dx =c_n \int_{\R^n} u(x) P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{v(x) - v(z)}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dz\, dx.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: Changing the roles of the variables we also have \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n} u(x) {\sqrtl} v(x)\, dx =c_n \int_{\R^n} u(z) P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{v(z) - v(x)}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dx\, dz.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n} u(x) {\sqrtl} v(x)\, dx =c_n \int_{\R^n} u(z) P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{v(z) - v(x)}{|x-z|^{n+1}}\, dx\, dz.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-094: Adding the previous two expressions and dividing by two, we obtain the desired result.
elements.sent-2411-095: Let us finish this short introduction to the square root of the Laplacian noticing the invariances satisfied by the operator, and how these invariances actually characterize it: \begin{lem Let $u\in C^2(\R^n)\cap L^\infty(\R^n)$.}

elements.sent-2411-307: The square root of the Laplacian is a particular instance of a more general class of nonlocal operators: the fractional Laplacian, denoted $\fls$ for $s\in(0,1)$, which is an operator of order $2s$.
elements.sent-2411-308: In this section we extend the properties introduced for ${\sqrtl}$ in Sections~\ref{sec:harm_extension -\ref{sec:explicit_solutions to the context of the fractional Laplacian $\fls$; we refer to \cite{AV19, BV, CLM20, Gar19, MRS for further properties and results.}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: When acting on a smooth function $u\in C^\infty_c(\R^n)$, we define the fractional Laplacian $\fls$ as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:fractional_Laplacian}\index{Fractional Laplacian} \begin{split} {\fls} u(x) := c_{n,s} P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+2s}}\, dy \,= c_{n,s} P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(z)}{|x-z|^{n+2s}}\, dz, \end{split}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:cns} c_{n,s} := 2^{2s} s \frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{n+2s}{2}\right)}{\Gamma(1-s)} \pi^{-\frac{n}{2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-162: \begin{equation}\label{eq:fractional_Laplacian}\index{Fractional Laplacian}  {\fls} u(x) := c_{n,s} P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(x+y)}{|y|^{n+2s}}\, dy \,= c_{n,s} P.V.\int_{\R^n} \frac{u(x) - u(z)}{|x-z|^{n+2s}}\, dz, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-311: As for the square root of the Laplacian ($s = \frac12$), due to concerns on integrability at the origin (when $s \ge \frac12$), we use the Principal Value of the integral.
elements.sent-2411-312: We also have the alternative definition that takes advantage of the symmetry of the kernel to obtain an expression with second order incremental quotients (that no longer needs $P.V.$): \begin{equation}{\fls} u(x) = \frac{c_{n,s}}{2}\int_{\R^n} \frac{2 u(x) - u(x+y)-u(x-y)}{|y|^{n+2s}}\, dy.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-313: Exactly as for ${\sqrtl}$, if one assumes $u\in C^2(\R^n)\cap L^\infty(\R^n)$ then we can evaluate $\fls u$.

elements.subsection-2411-012: L\'evy processes and infinitely divisible distributions
elements.sent-2411-392: A stochastic process $(X_t)_{t \in T}$ is a family of random variables in $\R^n$ (on a common probability space) indexed by $t\in T$.
elements.sent-2411-393: In this exposition we will consider $ T = [0, \infty)$ to be regarded as time.
elements.sent-2411-394: Stochastic processes are widely used as models of systems and phenomena that evolve in a random way, and appear in particular in many areas of physics, biology, or even finance.
elements.sent-2411-395: The Brownian motion is the most famous stochastic process, and together with the Poisson process, the Cauchy process, or the Gamma process, they belong to a wider class known as L\'evy processes.
elements.sent-2411-396: Let us start with a definition: \begin{defi \index{L\'evy process A stochastic process $(X_t)_{t\ge 0}$ on $\R^n$ is a L\'evy process if it satisfies the following properties:.}}
elements.sent-2411-397: In fact, given any L\'evy process $X_t$, there always exists a modification of $X_t$ (or a stochastically equivalent process) satisfying this property; see, e.g., \cite{Sat99 .}
elements.sent-2411-398: For this reason, we will also assume that our L\'evy processes are c\`adl\`ag.
elements.sent-2411-399: The study of L\'evy processes and their characterization is done by means of the concept of infinitely divisible distributions (which also play an important role for generalizations of the central limit theorem and in additive processes): \begin{defi [Infinitely divisible distributions]\index{Infinitely divisible distribution Let $\mu\in \mathcal{P (\R^n)$ be a probability measure.}}}
elements.sent-2411-400: We say that $\mu$ is infinitely divisible if, for any $m\in \N$, there exists $\mu_m\in \mathcal{P (\R^n)$ such that \begin{equation}\mu = \underbrace{\mu_m * \dots * \mu_m}_{m}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-401: Alternatively, we say that a random variable $X$ is infinitely divisible if, for any $m\in \N$, there exist a random variable $Y_m$ such that \begin{equation}X \overset{d}{=} Y^{(1)}_m +\dots Y^{(m)}_m\end{equation} where $Y^{(1) _m,\dots, Y^{(m) _m$ are independent copies of $Y_m$, and $\overset{d {=}$ denotes equality in distribution (that is, ${\rm Law (X)$ is an infinitely divisible distribution).. \end{defi.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-402: The most common infinitely divisible distribution is the Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-403: That is, if $X$ is a random variable with distribution $N(0, 1)$ (normal with zero mean and variance one), then for each $m$ the previous statement holds with $Y \sim N(0, 1/m)$.
elements.sent-2411-405: Observe that if $(X_t)_{t \ge 0}$ is a L\'evy process then, for any $t\ge 0$, the random variable $X_t$ (or its distribution) is infinitely divisible, since for every $m \in \N$ we have \begin{equation}X_t \overset{d}{=} \left(X_{\frac{t}{m}}-X_0\right) + \left(X_{t\frac{2}{m}}-X_{\frac{t}{m}}\right) + \dots+ \left(X_{t}-X_{t\frac{m-1}{m}}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-406: In fact, the relationship between L\'evy processes and infinitely divisible distributions is an equivalence (see \cite[Theorem 7.10]{Sat99 ): \begin{thm If $\mu\in \mathcal{P (\R^n)$ is an infinitely divisible distribution, then there is a L\'evy process $(X_t)_{t \ge 0}$ such that $X_1 \sim \mu$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-407: Moreover, if $(X_t')_{t\ge 0}$ is another L\'evy process such that $X_1 \overset{d {= X_1'$, then $(X_t)_{t\ge 0 \overset{d {= (X_t')_{t\ge 0}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-408: In this case, the process has continuous paths.
elements.sent-2411-409: Thanks to the previous result, we have that the characterization of L\'evy processes is equivalent to the characterization of infinitely divisible distributions.
elements.sent-2411-410: In the latter case, such characterization is accomplished through the L\'evy-Khintchine formula, which gives a representation of the characteristic function of any infinitely divisible measure.
elements.sent-2411-411: We recall that the characteristic function of a probability measure $\mu$, denoted $\hat{\mu}$, is its (inverse) Fourier transform: \begin{equation}\hat{\mu}(\xi) = \E_{Y\sim \mu}\left[e^{i \xi\cdot Y}\right] = \int_{\R^n} e^{i \xi\cdot y}\mu(dy).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-412: Then \begin{equation}\hat{\mu}(\xi) = e^{\Psi(\xi)},\end{equation} where $\Psi$ is the characteristic exponent, given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Psi} \Psi(\xi) = -\frac12 \xi\cdot A\xi + i b\cdot \xi + \int_{\R^n} \left(e^{i \xi\cdot y} - 1- i \xi\cdot y\chi_{B_1}(y)\right) \nu(dy).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-202: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Psi} \Psi(\xi) = -\frac12 \xi\cdot A\xi + i b\cdot \xi + \int_{\R^n} \left(e^{i \xi\cdot y} - 1- i \xi\cdot y\chi_{B_1}(y)\right) \nu(dy).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-414: Conversely, any $(A, b, \nu)$ with the previous properties generates an infinitely divisible distribution.
elements.sent-2411-415: This is because, for any $\xi, y\in \R^n$, \begin{equation}\big|e^{i\xi\cdot y} - 1\big|\le 2\quadin\quad \R^n\setminus B_1,\qquad\big|e^{i\xi\cdot y} - 1-i\xi\cdot y\big|\le C|\xi \cdot y|^2\quadin\quad B_1.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-419: When we take as infinitely divisible distribution the Gaussian distribution, $N(0, 1)$, then in $\Psi$ above we have $A = {\rm Id}$, $b = 0$, and $\nu \equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-420: The triple $(A, b, \nu)$ is called a generating triplet for $\mu$, and characterizes the infinitely divisible distribution $\mu$ (and hence, also characterizes the law of the corresponding L\'evy process).

elements.subsection-2411-017: Kernels comparable to the fractional Laplacian
elements.sent-2411-521: As such, it serves as a motivation to justify the following more general classes of L\'evy processes, that will be of importance throughout this book.
elements.sent-2411-522: A very typical and natural assumption in the study of integro-differential operators is to consider infinitesimal generators with L\'evy measures (or kernels) comparable to the one of the fractional Laplacian, see \eqref{eq:Kcompfls .}
elements.sent-2411-523: These are sometimes called stable-like operators (see \cite{BL02, BL02b, CH03, Sil06, Bas09, Kas09 ), and correspond to the $\mathcal L_0$ class of Caffarelli and Silvestre \cite{CS,CS2,CS3 .}}

elements.section-2411-012: Basic properties and notions of solution
elements.sent-2411-577: In this section we will prove some basic properties on the class of ope\-rators we consider, some of them analogous to those we have seen for the fractional Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-578: Moreover, we also introduce the notions of strong, weak, and distributional solution for these linear elliptic integro-differential operators.
elements.sent-2411-579: Let us start with a preliminary property on the class of kernels $\GL$ itself, given by Definition~\ref{defi:G .}
elements.sent-2411-580: We show here that the conditions \eqref{eq:Kellipt_gen_L -\eqref{eq:Kellipt_gen_l are indeed equivalent to \eqref{eq:GSLO (i.e., the Fourier symbol is comparable to the Fourier symbol of the fractional Laplacian).}}}
elements.sent-2411-581: In order to do that, we will use that condition \eqref{eq:Kellipt_gen_L implies (and up to a constant, is equivalent to) \begin{equation}\label{eq:prop_kernel} \int_{\R^n\setminus B_r} K(dy) \le C \Lambda r^{-2s}\quad\text{for all}\quad r > 0,\end{equation} for some $C$ depending only on $s$, since \begin{equation}\int_{\R^n\setminus B_r} K(dy) = \sum_{k\ge 0} \int_{B_{2^{k+1}r} \setminus B_{2^k r}} K(dy) \le r^{-2s}\sum_{k\ge 0} 2^{-2ks} = C r^{-2s}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-583: Indeed, \begin{equation} \int_{B_r} |y|^{2s+\alpha}K(dy) \le \sum_{k\ge 0}\int_{B_{r/2^{k}}\setminus B_{r/2^{k+1}}} (2^{-k}r)^{2s+\alpha}K(dy) \le \Lambda r^{\alpha} \sum_{k\ge 0} 2^{-k\alpha+2s} \le C \Lambda r^\alpha, \end{equation} and \begin{equation} \int_{B_r^c} |y|^{2s-\alpha}K(dy) \le \sum_{k\ge 0}\int_{B_{2^{k+1}r}\setminus B_{2^{k}r}} (2^{k+1}r)^{2s-\alpha}K(dy) \le \Lambda r^{-\alpha} \sum_{k\ge 0} 2^{2s-\alpha k -\alpha} \le C \Lambda r^{-\alpha}. \end{equation} Finally, by a similar reasoning we have that if \eqref{eq:prop_kernel_Calpha holds, then \begin{equation}\label{eq:prop_kernel4} \int_{B_\rho^c}|y|^{2s+\mu-\alpha} \big|D^mK(x-dy)-D^m K(x'-dy)\big| \le C [K]_\mu \rho^{-\alpha} |x-x'|^{\mu - m}\end{equation} for any $\alpha > 0$.}

elements.subsection-2411-024: Integration by parts and weak solutions
elements.sent-2411-681: In order to write it, we define the following symmetric and positive semi-definite bilinear form $ \langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_K:C_c^\infty(\R^n)\times C^\infty_c(\R^n)\to \R $ given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:bilin} \langle u, v \rangle_K := \frac12 \iint_{\R^n\times\R^n} \big(u(x) - u(x+y)\big)\big(v(x)-v(x+y)\big) K(dy)\, dx.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-682: Observe that, for notational simplicity only, in this section that we will sometimes assume \begin{equation}K(dy) = K(y)\, dy.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-683: In this case, the expression \eqref{eq:bilin has a symmetric representation as \begin{equation}\label{eq:bilin_sym} \langle u, v \rangle_K := \frac12 \iint_{\R^n\times\R^n} \big(u(x) - u(z)\big)\big(v(x)-v(z)\big) K(x-z)\, dx\, dz.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-686: We first observe that, for $u, v\in C^\infty_c(\R^n)$, $\langle u, v\rangle_K$ is well-defined, since the integrand is absolutely integrable.
elements.sent-2411-687: Indeed, \begin{equation} \frac12 \iint_{\R^n\times \R^n}\big|u(x+y) - u(x)\big|\big|v(x+y)-v(x)\big| K(dy)\, dx \quad \le \frac14 \iint_{\R^n\times \R^n}\big|u(x+y) - u(x)\big|^2 K(dy)\, dx \qquad +\frac14 \iint_{\R^n\times \R^n}\big|v(x+y) - v(x)\big|^2 K(dy)\, dx. \end{equation} Since, whenever ${\rm supp (u) \subset B_R$, \begin{equation}\big|u(x+y)-u(x)\big|^2\le C\|u\|_{C^2(\R^n)}\min\{1, |y|^2\}\chi_{B_R\cup B_R(-y)}(x),\end{equation} the previous integrals are finite (by \eqref{eq:Kint ) and $\langle u, v\rangle_K$ is well-defined.}}

elements.sent-2411-828: Super- and subsolutions to equations of the form $\L u=0 $ satisfy a maximum principle and a comparison principle.
elements.sent-2411-829: As for the classical case of the Laplacian $-\Delta$, the maximum principle essentially relies on the fact that $\L u(x_\circ) \ge 0$ whenever $u$ has a maximum at $x_\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-830: For local equations this is true for any local maximum, while in the nonlocal setting this is only true when $u$ has a global maximum at $x_\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-831: In this case, the inequality $\L u(x_\circ) \ge 0$ follows simply from the integral expression of $\L$ and the fact that $u(x_\circ)\ge u(x_\circ\pm y)$ for any\footnote{When $K > 0$ in $\R^n$, such argument already yields a strong maximum principle, since one has $\L u(x_\circ) > 0$ unless $u$ is constant.}
elements.sent-2411-832: However, for $\L \in \GL$, the strong maximum principle turns out to be more delicate, see subsection~\ref{ssec:strongmaximumprinciple .. $y \in \R^n$.}

elements.sent-2411-1102: The results we have established so far give a quite complete understanding of the interior regularity of solutions to linear and translation invariant equations of order $2s$.
elements.sent-2411-1103: The next very natural question is to understand what happens when the operators under consideration are not translation invariant, i.e., they have $x$-dependence.
elements.sent-2411-1104: For this, we have two different classes of operators: in non-divergence form and in divergence form:.
elements.sent-2411-1105: They correspond (in the limiting case $s=1$) to operators of the type \begin{equation}\L(u,x)= {\rm tr}(A(x)D^2 u)= \sum_{i,j=1}^n a_{ij}(x)\partial_{ij}u,\end{equation} and arise naturally when studying fully nonlinear elliptic equations.
elements.sent-2411-1106: Equations involving these operators cannot be studied by using weak solutions and energy functionals, and instead require the use of viscosity solutions (see Chapter \ref{ch:fully_nonlinear ).}
elements.sent-2411-1107: We can, however, establish a priori interior regularity estimates (Schauder estimates).
elements.sent-2411-1108: The kernels we consider, $K(x, y)\, dy$, can in general be measures $K(x, dy)$, that will satisfy \eqref{eq:nu_cond for each $x\in \R^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-1109: In that case, the operators are given by \eqref{x-dependence-L-nu .}
elements.sent-2411-1110: They correspond (in the limiting case $s=1$) to operators of the type \begin{equation}\L(u,x)= {\rm div}\big(A(x)\nabla u\big)= \sum_{i,j=1}^n \partial_i\big(a_{ij}(x)\partial_{j}u\big),\end{equation} and arise naturally in the Calculus of Variations.
elements.sent-2411-1111: Equations involving these operators are studied by using weak solutions and integration by parts, and some results (such as the existence of solutions and maximum principles) may be established similarly to the ones we proved in this chapter.
elements.sent-2411-1112: Notice that, as before, the kernel $K(x,z)\, dz$ could actually be a general measure $K(x,dz)$.
elements.sent-2411-1113: In that case, the previous expressions are given by \eqref{divergence-form0 -\eqref{divergence-form1 .}}

elements.sent-2411-1537: We say that \begin{equation}\dr_\Omega:\R^n\to \R_{\ge 0}\end{equation} is a regularized distance if $\dr_\Omega\in C^\infty(\Omega)\cap C^\beta(\overline{\Omega )$, there exists a constant $C$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:compOmega} d_\Omega \le \dr_\Omega \le C d_\Omega \quad\text{in}\quad \R^n,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:compOmega2} |D^k \dr_\Omega| \le C_k \dr_\Omega^{\beta-k} \quad\text{in}\quad \Omega,\quad\text{for all}\quad k \ge \beta\end{equation} for some $C_k$ depending only on $\Omega$, $k$, and $\beta$.}
elements.sent-2411-1538: If $\Omega$ is Lipschitz domain, we say that $\dr_\Omega:\R^n\to \R_{\ge 0}$ is a regularized distance if $\dr_\Omega\in C^\infty(\Omega)\cap C^{0,1 (\overline{\Omega )$, and \eqref{eq:compOmega -\eqref{eq:compOmega2 hold for $\beta = 1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1539: Indeed, after flattening the boundary it is enough to show it when $\Omega = \{x_n > 0\}$ locally.
elements.sent-2411-1540: In this case, we can write \begin{equation}\frac{u}{x_n} = \int_0^1 u_{x_n}(x', tx_n)\, dt \in C^{\beta-1}\end{equation} for $u = d_\Omega, \dr_\Omega$, since $u$ is $C^\beta$ in $\Omega$, and $u_{x_n}\in C^{\beta-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-1541: Hence, since furthermore $u/{x_n}$ is uniformly positive and bounded in $\Omega$, we get that \begin{equation}\frac{d_\Omega}{\dr_\Omega} =\frac{d_\Omega}{x_n}\frac{x_n}{\dr_\Omega} \in C^{\beta-1}(\overline{N_\delta}),\end{equation} as claimed.
elements.sent-2411-1542: Indeed, since $w := {\dr_\Omega /{d_\Omega}\in C^\alpha(B_{r_\circ )$ for some $r_\circ$ universal (by Remark~\ref{rem:quotient ), and $1\le w \le C$ in $\Omega$, we also have that $w^s\in C^\alpha(B_{r_\circ )$ and \begin{equation}\left\|\frac{\dr^s_\Omega}{d^s_\Omega} - w(0)\right\|_{L^\infty(B_r)}\le Cr^{\alpha}\quad\Rightarrow \quad \left\|{\dr^s_\Omega}- w(0){d^s_\Omega} \right\|_{L^\infty(B_r)}\le Cr^{s+\alpha},\end{equation} for any $r\in (0, r_\circ)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1543: By the triangle inequality we now have that the statement holds for $\dr_\Omega$ if and only if it holds for $d_\Omega$.

elements.sent-2411-1648: In this chapter we study fully nonlinear equations of the type \begin{equation}\label{ch3-0} \mathcal I u = 0 \quad in\quad \Omega\subset\R^n,\end{equation} where $\mathcal I$ is an operator of the form \begin{equation}\label{ch3-1} \mathcal I w := \inf_{\gamma\in \Gamma} \big\{ -\L_\gamma w\big\}\qquad or\qquad \mathcal I w := \inf_{b\in \mathcal B}\sup_{a\in\mathcal A} \big\{ -\L_{ab} w\big\},\end{equation} and $\L_\gamma$ or $\L_{ab}$ are linear integro-differential elliptic operators of order $2s$.

elements.sent-2411-1649: When $s=1$, these correspond to fully nonlinear elliptic PDE, \begin{equation}F(D^2 u) = 0 \quad in\quad \Omega\subset\R^n,\end{equation} and their study has been a major research direction since the second half of the 20th century, with many important contributions by Nirenberg, Krylov, Safonov, Evans, Caffarelli, and many others; see \cite{CC, NTV14, FR4 for more details.}

elements.sent-2411-1651: Here we will present first the basic results concerning the existence and uniqueness of (viscosity) solutions for these equations, to then establish some of the main known regularity results.
elements.sent-2411-1652: Let $\Gamma=\{1,...,N\}$, and $\{X_t^i\}_{i\in\Gamma}$ be a finite collection of L\'evy processes, with infinitesimal generators $\L_i$.
elements.sent-2411-1653: Given a bounded smooth domain $\Omega\subset \R^n$, and a payoff function $g\in C(\R^n\setminus \Omega)$, we saw in subsection~\ref{subsect-2.1.4 that if we consider the process $x+X_t^i$ (i.e., a ``particle'' starts at $x\in \Omega$ and moves randomly according to $X_t^i$), then the expected payoff of the first hitting time~$\tau_x$ (the first time the particle falls outside $\Omega$), denoted $u_i(x)$, is given by the solution of \begin{equation}\left\{ {rcll} \L_i u_i = 0 \quadin\quad \Omega u_i = g \quadin\quad \R^n\setminus \Omega.  \right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1654: Here, we consider instead the following control problem: suppose that at any time $t$ we can choose to change the parameter $i\in \Gamma$ so that the particle that has reached the position $x$ at time $t$ must then move according to $X_t^i$ during a very small time interval $(t,t+\delta)$.
elements.sent-2411-1655: We get a process $X_t^{\gamma}$, where $\gamma:\Omega\to \Gamma$ may actually change from point to point.
elements.sent-2411-1656: Our goal is to minimize\footnote{Notice that, if we wanted to maximize the payoff, we could simply consider $-g$ instead of $g$.}
elements.sent-2411-1657: The minimum expected payoff is then given by \begin{equation}u(x) := \inf_{\substack{all possible choices of  \gamma:\Omega\to \Gamma}} \E[g(X_{\tau_x}^{\gamma})].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1658: Observe that, since we can always choose to continue with a constant $\gamma\equiv i$, we have $u(x) \le \E\left[u(x+X_t^i)\right]$ for every $x\in \Omega$ (and every $i\in \Gamma$), and from \eqref{eq:Ptdef -\eqref{eq:def-infinit-generat we get $-\L_i u \ge 0$ in $\Omega$ for all $i\in \Gamma$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1659: On the other hand, assuming that $u$ is regular enough, one can show that for short time intervals we will have $u(x) = \E\left[u(x+X_t^i)\right]+o(t)$ for some $i\in \Gamma$, so that (again by \eqref{eq:Ptdef -\eqref{eq:def-infinit-generat ) at each $x\in \Omega$ we have that $\L_i u(x) = 0$ for some $i\in \Gamma$.}}

elements.sent-2411-1677: Namely, we consider operators of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lu1} \begin{split} \L u(x) = {\rm P.V.} \int_{\R^n}\big(u(x)-u(x+y)\big)K(y) \, dy = \frac12 \int_{\R^n}\big(2u(x)-u(x+y)-u(x-y)\big)K(y)\,dy, \end{split}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:Kint1} K(y) = K(-y)\qquad\text{in}\quad \R^n,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:compdef} 0< \frac{\lambda}{|y|^{n+2s}}\le K(y) \le \frac{\Lambda}{|y|^{n+2s}}\qquad\text{in}\quad \R^n,\end{equation} and we define the classes $\LL_s(\lambda, \Lambda)$ and $\LL_s(\lambda, \Lambda;\mu)$ for $\mu > 0$ as follows (cf.~Definitions~\ref{defi:G and \ref{defi:Gmu ): \begin{defi Let $s\in (0, 1)$, $0< \lambda \le \Lambda$, and $\mu > 0$ with $\mu\notin\N$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-1717: In the previous chapter, in subsection~\ref{ssec:existence_weak we showed the existence of solutions to (linear) integro-differential elliptic equations in a variational way (i.e., by minimizing an energy functional), and in order to do that we introduced the notion of weak solution.}
elements.sent-2411-1718: In this chapter, however, the same method does not apply: in general, fully nonlinear equations do not have a variational formulation (in particular, they do not come from an energy functional).
elements.sent-2411-1719: To construct solutions, therefore, we will need to rely on other methods, and in this case, it will be through a defining factor in elliptic problems: the comparison principle.
elements.sent-2411-1720: We will use Perron's method to prove existence of solutions, and in order to do that we need a new generalized notion of solution: viscosity solutions.
elements.sent-2411-1721: Notice that if the new notion were too restrictive, then we might be able to prove a comparison principle (and uniqueness of solutions) but it might be difficult to prove existence.
elements.sent-2411-1722: Otherwise, if we relaxed the notion of solution too much, we might be able to prove existence, but within such a general class that uniqueness might not be possible.
elements.sent-2411-1723: Hence, one has to find the right balance.
elements.sent-2411-1724: In this case, such a balance is found with the notion of viscosity solution in Definition~\ref{defi:viscosity below, which allows the proofs of existence, uniqueness, and stability of solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-1725: Originally introduced by Crandall and Lions in 1983 in the study of first-order equations, \cite{CL83 , in 1988 Jensen showed that the concept is also well-posed for second order elliptic equations, \cite{Jen88 , and since then it has become prevalent in the analysis of elliptic problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-1726: In the context of integro-differential equations, the basic theory of viscosity solutions was developed by Caffarelli and Silvestre in \cite{CS .}
elements.sent-2411-1727: This section is based partly on \cite{CS, CS3, S-viscosity, FR4 ; see also \cite{Mou and \cite{BCI,BCI2,BI .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1728: In the following, we recall the notion of semi-continuity: \begin{defi \index{Semi-continuity A function $u$ is lower semi-continuous at a point $x_\circ\in \R^n$ if $u(x_\circ) > -\infty$ and \begin{equation}\liminf_{x\to x_\circ} u(x) \ge u(x_\circ).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1729: Similarly, it is upper semi-continuous at $x_\circ \in \R^n$ if $u (x_\circ) < \infty$ and \begin{equation}\limsup_{x\to x_\circ } u(x) \le u(x_\circ).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1730: Of course, $u$ is continuous at $x_\circ$ if and only if it is both upper and lower semi-continuous.
elements.sent-2411-1731: Given any set $D\subset \R^n$, we say that $u$ is lower (resp.
elements.sent-2411-1732: In particular, if $D$ is open, then this is equivalent to $u$ being lower (resp.
elements.sent-2411-1733: We say that $u\in {\rm USC ({\Omega )\cap L^1_{\omega_s (\R^n)$ is a viscosity subsolution to \eqref{eq:defivisc in $\Omega$, and we denote \begin{equation}\I (u, x) \ge f(x)\quad in\quad \Omega,\end{equation} if for any $x\in \Omega$ and any neighborhood of $x$ in $\Omega$, $N_x\subset \Omega$, and for any test function $\phi\in L^1_{\omega_s (\R^n)$ such that $\phi\in C^2(N_x)$, $\phi(x) = u(x)$, and $\phi \ge u$ in all of $\R^n$, we have $\I (\phi,x) \ge f(x)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1734: We say that $u\in {\rm LSC (\Omega)\cap L^1_{\omega_s (\R^n)$ is a viscosity supersolution to \eqref{eq:defivisc in $\Omega$, and we denote \begin{equation}\I (u, x) \le f(x)\quad in\quad \Omega,\end{equation} if for any $x\in \Omega$ and any neighborhood of $x$ in $\Omega$, $N_x\subset \Omega$, and for any test function $\phi\in L^1_{\omega_s (\R^n)$ such that $\phi\in C^2(N_x)$, $\phi(x) = u(x)$, and $\phi \le u$ in all of $\R^n$, we have $\I (\phi,x) \le f(x)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1735: We say that $u\in C({\Omega )\cap L^1_{\omega_s (\R^n)$ is a viscosity solution to \eqref{eq:defivisc in $\Omega$, and we denote $\I(u, x) = f(x)$ in $\Omega$, if it is both a viscosity subsolution and supersolution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1736: In particular, there might be points $x\in \Omega$ at which there is no function $\phi\in C^2$ touching $u$ at $x_\circ$ (from above and/or below); this is allowed by the previous definition.

elements.sent-2411-2499: In this chapter we have established the main known interior regularity results for solutions to fully nonlinear integro-differential equations of order $2s$.
elements.sent-2411-2500: In particular, two of the main results yield that solutions to concave equations \begin{equation}\label{question3.1} \inf_{a\in \A}\big\{-\L_a u+c_a\big\} =0\quadin\quad B_1\end{equation} are $C^{\max\{1,2s\}+\alpha}$ in $B_1$ for all $s\in (0,1)$.

elements.sent-2411-2502: An important feature of the regularity theory developed by Caffarelli and Silvestre in \cite{CS,CS2,CS3 is that the constants in all the estimates that they establish do not blow-up as $s\uparrow1$.}

elements.sent-2411-2516: Throughout this chapter we mainly studied the interior regularity of solutions for fully nonlinear operators of the form \begin{equation}\I u :=\inf_{a\in \A}\sup_{b\in\B}\big\{-\L_{ab} u+c_{ab}\big\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2517: It is then natural to wonder what can be said about the boundary regularity of solutions to Dirichlet problems of the form \begin{equation}\left\{ {rcll} \I u = f \quadin\quad \Omega u = 0 \quadin\quad \R^n\setminus\Omega. \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-2518: The boundary regularity theory for fully nonlinear equations was developed by the second author and Serra in \cite{RS-Duke , where we proved that, if the kernels of the operators $\L_{ab}$ are homogeneous, i.e., \begin{equation}K_{ab}(y)=\frac{K_{ab}(y/|y|)}{|y|^{n+2s}}\end{equation} (as in in Sections \ref{sec:bdryregularity and \ref{sec:higherorder ), then the following holds: \begin{equation}\left\{ {l} \partial\Omega\in C^{2,\alpha} f\in C^{1+\alpha-s}(\overline{\Omega}) \text{$K_{ab}|_{\mathbb S^{n-1}}$ are ``regular enough''}  \right. \quad\Longrightarrow\qquad \frac{u}{d^s}\in C^{1+\alpha}(\overline{\Omega}),\end{equation} for some small $\alpha>0$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-1328: \begin{equation}\left\{ {l} \partial\Omega\in C^{2,\alpha} f\in C^{1+\alpha-s}(\overline{\Omega}) \text{$K_{ab}|_{\mathbb S^{n-1}}$ are ``regular enough''}  \right. \quad\Longrightarrow\qquad \frac{u}{d^s}\in C^{1+\alpha}(\overline{\Omega}),}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-2519: The exponent $\alpha$ comes from a related estimate for equations with bounded measurable coefficients with homogeneous kernels; see \cite[Proposition~1.1]{RS-Duke .}

elements.sent-2411-2537: We have a L\'evy process $X_t$ in $\R^n$ and some given payoff function $\varphi:\R^n\rightarrow\R$.

elements.sent-2411-2540: This means that the value function in any optimal stopping problem solves an obstacle problem of the type \eqref{obstacle-motivation1 .}
elements.sent-2411-2541: In the context of mathematical finance, this type of problem appears as a pricing model for American options \cite{CT , where the function $\varphi$ represents the option's payoff, and the contact set $\{v=\varphi\}$ is the exercise region.}
elements.sent-2411-2542: Notice that, in this context, the most important unknown to understand is precisely the contact set, i.e., one wants to find and/or understand the two regions $\{v=\varphi\}$ (in which we should exercise the option) and $\{v>\varphi\}$ (in which we should wait and not exercise the option yet).
elements.sent-2411-2543: The free boundary is the separating interface between these two regions.

elements.sent-2411-2545: Indeed, many different phenomena in physics, biology, or material science, give rise to models with interacting particles or individuals.
elements.sent-2411-2546: In such a context, the 2D mathematical model is usually the following; see \cite{BCLR,Serfaty .}
elements.sent-2411-2547: We are given a repulsive-attractive interaction potential $W\in L^1_{\rm loc (\R^2)$, and its associated interaction energy \begin{equation}E[\mu]:=\frac12\int_{\R^2}\int_{\R^2}W(x-y)d\mu(x)d\mu(y),\end{equation} where $\mu$ is any probability measure in $\R^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-2548: The potential $W$ is repulsive when the particles or individuals are very close, and attractive when they are far from each other.
elements.sent-2411-2549: A typical assumption is that near the origin we have \begin{equation}\label{W} W(z) \asymp \frac{1}{|z|^{\beta}}\quad for\quad z\sim 0,\end{equation} for some $\beta\in(0,2)$.
elements.sent-2411-2550: Moreover, it would usually grow at infinity, say $W(z)\asymp |z|^{\gamma}$ for $z\gg 1$.
elements.sent-2411-2551: An important question to be understood is that of the regularity of minimizers, i.e., the regularity properties of the measures $\mu_0$ which minimize the interaction energy $E[\mu]$.
elements.sent-2411-2552: It turns out that the minimizer $\mu_0$ is given by $\mu_0=\L v$, where $v(x):=\int_{\R^2 W(x-y)d\mu_0(y)$ satisfies the {obstacle problem \eqref{obstacle-motivation1 for a certain operator $\L$ and a certain obstacle $\varphi$ that depend on $W$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-2553: When $W$ satisfies \eqref{W (as well as some extra conditions), such operator~$\L$ turns out to be an integro-differential operator with a kernel \begin{equation}K(z)\asymp \frac{1}{|z|^{n+2s}}\quad for\quad z\sim 0,\end{equation} with $n=2$ and $2s=2-\beta\in (0,2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-2554: Therefore, understanding the regularity of minimizers $\mu_0$ of the interaction energy $E[\mu]$ is equivalent to understanding the regularity of solutions and free boundaries in obstacle problems for integro-differential operators.
elements.sent-2411-2555: In this setting, the contact set $\{v=\varphi\}$ is the support of the minimizer $\mu_0$.

elements.sent-2411-2557: This is a classical free boundary problem which dates back to 1933 and arises in a model of linear elasticity \cite{Sig59 .}
elements.sent-2411-2558: The problem consists in finding the elastic equilibrium configuration of a 3D elastic body, resting on a rigid frictionless surface and subject only to its mass forces.
elements.sent-2411-2559: The problem leads to a system of variational inequalities for the displacement vector in $\R^3$, which can be transformed to a scalar function $v$ that solves \eqref{obstacle-motivation1 for $\L=\sqrt{-\Delta}$; see \cite{CDV22, RS-thin,F-survey for more details.}}
elements.sent-2411-2560: On the other hand, the Signorini problem gained further attention in the seventies due to its connection to mechanics and biology, where it models the process of osmosis in the study of {semipermeable membranes .}
elements.sent-2411-2561: We refer to the classical book of Duvaut and Lions \cite{DL for more details about these models.}
elements.sent-2411-2562: Our goal is to study the regularity properties of solutions and free boundaries in the following class of obstacle problems: given $s\in(0,1)$ and $n>2s$ we consider solutions of \begin{equation}\label{obst-pb} \min\big\{\L v,\,v-\varphi\big\} = 0 \quadin\quad \R^n,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{obst-pb2} \lim_{|x|\to\infty} v(x)=0,\end{equation} with $\varphi\in C^\infty_c(\R^n)$, and where $\L$ is a L\'evy operator of the form \begin{equation}\label{obst-op1} \begin{split} \L u(x) = {\rm P.V.}\int_{\R^n}\big(u(x)-u(x+y)\big)K(y)dy = \frac12\int_{\R^n}\big(2u(x)-u(x+y)-u(x-y)\big)K(y)dy , \end{split}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{obst-op2} K(y)=K(-y) \qquadand\qquad \frac{\lambda}{|y|^{n+2s}} \leq K(y) \leq \frac{\Lambda}{|y|^{n+2s}},\end{equation} and $0<\lambda\leq \Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-2563: As in Chapter~\ref{ch:fully_nonlinear , we denote by $\LLL$ the set of operators for which \eqref{obst-op1 -\eqref{obst-op2 hold (see Definition~\ref{defi:LL ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2564: We will moreover assume that $\L$ is a stable operator, that is, \begin{equation}\label{obst-op3} $K$ is homogeneous, i.e., \quad K(y) = \frac{K(y/|y|)}{|y|^{n+2s}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2565: Notice that \eqref{obst-pb (or, equivalently, \eqref{obstacle2-motivation ) is a free boundary problem, in the sense that there are two unknowns: the solution $v$, and the set $\{v>\varphi\}$ where the equation $\L v=0$ holds.}}
elements.sent-2411-2566: The boundary of such (a priori unknown) set is the so-called free boundary $\partial\{v>\varphi\}$.
elements.sent-2411-2567: The main questions in this context are the following:.
elements.sent-2411-2568: What is the optimal regularity of solutions $v$ of \eqref{obst-pb ?.}
elements.sent-2411-2569: What can we say about the structure and regularity of their free boundaries?.
elements.sent-2411-2570: These are the questions that we tackle in this chapter.

elements.section-2411-023: Boundary Harnack in Lipschitz (and more general) domains

elements.section-2411-024: Regularity of free boundaries near regular points
elements.sent-2411-2733: The goal of this section is to establish the main free boundary regularity results for the obstacle problem, \eqref{obst-pb .}
elements.sent-2411-2734: The first and main result is the following, which gives a dichotomy between regular and degenerate points, and establishes the $C^{1,\gamma}$ regularity of free boundaries near regular points.
elements.sent-2411-2735: Notice that now we require the kernels to be homogeneous (or the operators, to be stable operators).
elements.sent-2411-2736: There exists $\gamma > 0$ depending only on $n$, $s$, $\lambda$, and $\Lambda$, such that the following holds.
elements.sent-2411-2737: Let $\varphi\in C^{2,\vartheta _c(\R^n)$, with $\vartheta>\max\{2s-1,0\}$, and let $v$ be the solution to the obstacle problem \eqref{obst-pb -\eqref{obst-pb2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-2738: Then, near any free boundary point $x_\circ\in \partial\{v>\varphi\}$ we have.
elements.sent-2411-2739: Moreover, the set of (regular) points $x_\circ$ satisfying (i) is an open subset of the free boundary, and it is locally a $C^{1,\gamma}$ manifold.
elements.sent-2411-2740: This result was first established in \cite{ACS for the operator $\L =\sqrt{-\Delta}$, then in \cite{CSS in case $\L =(-\Delta)^s$ for all $s\in(0,1)$, and finally in \cite{CRS for general operators of the form \eqref{obst-op1 -\eqref{obst-op2 -\eqref{obst-op3 ; see also \cite{FR2,FRS for the case of non-symmetric operators.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2741: On the other hand, we have the following higher regularity result, which yields that free boundaries are actually $C^\infty$ near regular points.
elements.sent-2411-2742: Assume in addition \begin{equation}\varphi\in C^\infty_c(\R^n)\qquad and\qquad K|_{\mathbb S^{n-1}} \in C^\infty(\S^{n-1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2743: Then, the set of regular free boundary points is locally a $C^\infty$ manifold.
elements.sent-2411-2744: Its proof is completely independent from that of Theorem \ref{thm:FB , as we will see later on.}
elements.sent-2411-2745: On the other hand, after we prove Theorem \ref{thm:FB we will establish the optimal $C^{1+s}$ regularity of solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-2746: More precisely, combining a quantitative version of the free boundary regularity result with an iterative argument from \cite{CFR,FRS , we will prove that \begin{equation}\|v\|_{C^{1,s}(\R^n)} \leq C\|\varphi\|_{C^{2,\vartheta}(\R^n)},\end{equation} with $C$ depending only on $n$, $s$, $\lambda$ and $\Lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-2747: This will be done in Section~\ref{sec-optimal-reg .}
elements.sent-2411-2748: As said before, the main goal of this section is to prove Theorem \ref{thm:FB .}
elements.sent-2411-2749: We first give an overview of the whole argument, to then provide a detailed proof in several steps.
elements.sent-2411-2750: To establish Theorem \ref{thm:FB , we consider \begin{equation}u:=v-\varphi,\end{equation} which satisfies \begin{equation}u\geq0\qquad and \qquad D^2 u \ge -C_\circ \,{\rm{Id}}\quad in\quad \R^n,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{rgnn2} \L u=f\quad in\quad \{u>0\} \qquad and \qquad \L u\geq f \quad in \quad \R^n,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\|f\|_{{\rm Lip}(\R^n)} \leq C_\circ\qquad and\qquad \|u\|_{{\rm Lip}(\R^n)} \le C_\circ.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-1438: \begin{equation}\label{rgnn2} \L u=f\quad in\quad \{u>0\} \qquad and \qquad \L u\geq f \quad in \quad \R^n,\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-3042: In this appendix, we state some useful properties about H\"older spaces.
elements.sent-2411-3043: Recall that, given $\alpha\in(0,1]$, the H\"older space $C^{0,\alpha (\Omega)$ is the set of functions $u\in C(\Omega)$ such that \begin{equation}[u]_{C^{0,\alpha}({\Omega})} := \sup_{\substack{x,y\in\Omega x\neq y}}\frac{\bigl|u(x)-u(y)\bigr|}{|x-y|^\alpha}<\infty.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-3045: When $\alpha=1$, this is the usual space of Lipschitz functions.
elements.sent-2411-3046: More generally, we define: \begin{defi [H\"older spaces] \index{H\"older spaces@Holder spaces Given $k\in\mathbb N$ and $\alpha\in(0,1]$, the space $C^{k,\alpha (\Omega)$ is the set of functions $u\in C^k(\Omega)$ such that the following norm is finite \begin{equation}\|u\|_{C^{k,\alpha}(\Omega)} :=\sum_{j=1}^k\|D^ju\|_{L^\infty(\Omega)}+\sup_{\substack{x,y\in\Omega x\neq y}}\frac{\bigl|D^ku(x)-D^ku(y)\bigr|}{|x-y|^\alpha} = \|u\|_{C^k(\Omega)} + [D^k u]_{C^{0,\alpha}({\Omega})}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3047: Here, we can take any equivalent definition for the norms of $D^j u$, for example $|D^j u| = \sum_{|a| = j |\partial^a u|$, where $a = (a_1,\dots,a_n)\in \N_0^n$ is a multi-index.}
elements.sent-2411-3048: When $\beta>0$ is not an integer, we denote $C^\beta(\Omega):=C^{k,\alpha (\Omega)$, where $\beta=k+\alpha$, with $k\in\mathbb N$, and $\alpha\in(0,1)$.}

elements.sent-2411-3334: We next want to construct supersolutions in Lipschitz (and more general) domains.

elements.sent-2411-3341: Using the previous lemma, we can now establish the following.

elements.sent-2411-3345: Since $|D^2\dr|\leq Cd^{-1}$ in $\Omega$ and $\dr\asymp d$, a direct computation shows that \begin{equation}\|\dr^\eps - \dr^\eps(x_\circ)\chi_{\Omega}\|_{C^2(B_{r_\circ})} \leq C\varepsilon r_\circ^{\eps-2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3346: Thus, we find \begin{equation}\big|\L(\dr^\eps-\dr^\eps(x_\circ)\chi_{\Omega})(x_\circ)\big| \leq C\eps r_\circ^{\eps-2s} + \Lambda\int_{\R^n\setminus B_{r_\circ}} \big|\dr^\eps-\dr^\eps(x_\circ)\chi_{\Omega}\big|\frac{dx}{|x-x_\circ|^{n+2s}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3347: In order to bound the last integral, let us fix $\eta,\gamma >0$ small, and $M>1$ large, to be chosen later, and define the following subsets of $\Omega\setminus B_{r_\circ (x_\circ)$, \begin{equation}A_\circ := \big\{ (1-\eta)\dr^\eps(x_\circ) < \dr^\eps < (1+\eta)\dr^\eps(x_\circ)\big\}\setminus B_{r_\circ}(x_\circ),\end{equation} \begin{equation}D_\circ := \big\{ 0 < \dr^\eps < (1-\eta)\dr^\eps(x_\circ)\big\} \subset \big\{0<\dr < \gamma r_\circ\big\},\end{equation} \begin{equation}E_\circ := \big\{ \dr^\eps > (1+\eta)\dr^\eps(x_\circ)\big\} \subset \Omega\setminus B_{Mr_\circ}(x_\circ),\end{equation} where the inclusions hold provided that $\eps>0$ is small enough (depending only on $\eta$, $\gamma$, and $M$).}
elements.sent-2411-3348: In particular, in the last inclusion we are using that if $x\in E_\circ$, then \begin{equation}c(1+\eta)^\eps r_\circ < \dr(x) \le C d(x) \le C\left(|x-x_\circ|+ r_\circ\right),\end{equation} by the triangle inequality, so that taking $\eps > 0$ small enough (depending on $\eta$ and $M$) we can make sure that $E_\circ \subset \Omega\setminus B_{Mr_\circ (x_\circ)$.}
elements.sent-2411-3349: Thus, we have \begin{equation}\int_{A_\circ} \frac{\big|\dr^\eps-\dr^\eps(x_\circ)\chi_{\Omega}\big|}{|x-x_\circ|^{n+2s}}\, dx \leq \int_{\Omega\setminus B_{r_\circ}(x_\circ)} C\eta r_\circ^\eps\frac{dx}{|x-x_\circ|^{n+2s}} \leq C\eta r_\circ^{\eps-2s},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\int_{E_\circ} \frac{\big|\dr^\eps-\dr^\eps(x_\circ)\chi_{\Omega}\big|}{|x-x_\circ|^{n+2s}}\, dx \leq \int_{\Omega\setminus B_{M r_\circ}(x_\circ)} \dr^\eps(x)\frac{dx}{|x-x_\circ|^{n+2s}} \leq C(Mr_\circ)^{\eps-2s}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-3350: It only remains to bound the integral over $D_\circ$.

elements.sent-2411-3415: Hence, the results in Lemmas~\ref{barrier-chi-Omega-2 and \ref{supersolution-d-eps-2 are also true in a Lipschitz domain with a universally small Lipschitz constant (depending only on $\lambda$, and $\Lambda$).}}
elements.sent-2411-3417: Let us introduce some of the notation that is used throughout the book.
elements.sent-2411-3418: Its value can change from line to line.
elements.sent-2411-3420: We say that $\Omega\subset\R^n$ is a domain if it is an open connected set.
elements.sent-2411-3422: Given a measure $\mu(dx)$ in $\R^n$, and for a given $x_\circ\in \R^n$, and $r\in \R\setminus\{0\}$, we denote by $\mu(x_\circ + r\, dx)$ the measure $\tilde \mu(dx)$ such that $\tilde\mu(B) = \mu(x_\circ + r B)$ for all Borel sets $B\subset \R^n$.. {\bf Specific notation.}
